Core Claims: Students learn better when instruction matches preferred sensory modality (Visual, Auditory, Read/Write, Kinesthetic)
Key Studies: Pashler et al. (2008) - 1,327 citations; Indriyani & Nurmasitah (2025) - no significant correlation (ρ=0.018, p=0.916); Sule et al. (2021) - no significant performance difference across VARK styles
Platform Recommendation: DO NOT USE for personalization decisions. Preferences exist but matching instruction to preference does not improve outcomes.

Core Claims: Intelligence is plural; teaching to 'dominant intelligences' improves outcomes
Key Studies: Davis et al. (2011); Rousseau (2021) - classified as neuromyth; limited psychometric validation
Platform Recommendation: DO NOT USE for instructional design. Educational philosophy value only.

Core Claims: Individual differences in optimal instructional methods exist and can be assessed
Key Studies: Pashler et al. (2008) - 'virtually no evidence' for meshing hypothesis; Kirschner (2017) - myth propagation warning
Platform Recommendation: DO NOT USE. No adequate evidence base for educational practice.

Core Claims: Instructional design should manage working memory limits; reduce extraneous load, optimize germane load
Key Studies: Sweller (2016) - 57 citations; Kala & Ayas (2023) - effective learning with high element interactivity; Chen & Kalyuga (2020) - spacing and CLT integration
Platform Recommendation: ESSENTIAL FOUNDATION. Apply split-attention, modality, worked example, and element interactivity principles universally.

Core Claims: Distributed practice over time improves long-term retention more than massed practice
Key Studies: Kang & Pashler (2011) - 173 citations; YeckehZaare et al. (2019) - motivating spaced tool; Cao & Carvalho (2025) - adaptive spaced retrieval in Algebra I
Platform Recommendation: IMPLEMENT. Use SM2 algorithm or LSTM-based models for optimal interval scheduling.

Core Claims: Active recall strengthens memory more than passive review
Key Studies: Agarwal et al. (2017) meta-analysis; Gao et al. (2025) - explanation strategies review; Balaganesan & Sethuraman (2024) - preventing knowledge loss
Platform Recommendation: IMPLEMENT. Build in low-stakes quizzing and self-explanation prompts.

Core Claims: Hidden Markov Model tracks knowledge state (learned/unlearned) for skills
Key Studies: Ben David et al. (2016) - classroom sequencing; MacHardy & Pardos (2015) - video evaluation; Shimada & Okada (2023) - reliability coefficient
Platform Recommendation: RECOMMENDED. Transparent, interpretable. Use for skill mastery tracking and at-risk identification.

Core Claims: LSTM/RNN predicts student performance from interaction sequences
Key Studies: Chen et al. (2018) - prerequisite-driven DKT (120 citations); Minn et al. (2018) - dynamic classification (115 citations); Mai et al. (2025) - transformer-Bayesian hybrid (0.847 AUC)
Platform Recommendation: RECOMMENDED. Superior predictive accuracy but less interpretable than BKT. Use for adaptive difficulty and personalized pathways.

Core Claims: Students must achieve criterion (80-90%) before progressing
Key Studies: Bloom (1968) - 1-sigma improvement; Sutiawan et al. (2025) - Cohen's d=1.2 for backpropagation; Dhanil et al. (2024) - meta-analysis g=0.78
Platform Recommendation: ESSENTIAL. Implement criterion-referenced progression with remediation loops.

Core Claims: Personalized content selection improves engagement and learning outcomes
Key Studies: Agrawal et al. (2022) - RCT: 60% content consumption increase, 14% overall usage increase; heavier users benefit more
Platform Recommendation: RECOMMENDED. Implement with caveats—benefits skew toward heavy users with niche preferences.
